tldr: Headlines across 25 outlet-country buckets converge on a single contested claim: Trump declares the US-Iran war 'over' and a deal near-ready, while Tehran says nothing is final. A parallel headline layer covers active military escalation — drone shootdowns, retaliatory missile strikes, and Hormuz blockade — that contradicts the peace optimism. A third strand foregrounds Kharg Island oil seizure as Trump's primary leverage threat, tying military escalation to energy-market stakes.

frames.sub_frame: Trump claims deal; Tehran says not final

Tehran says 'nothing' finalised after Trump claims deal

Vows to Hit Iran 'VERY HARD' on First Night of World Cup
'Netanyahu was not warned': Israeli PM blindsided as Trump

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to link Trump's Iran strike threats to the FIFA World Cup opening night, deploying an irony frame ('FIFA Peace Prize Recipient Vows to Hit Iran VERY HARD') absent from all other headline coverage.
Only outlet to quantify Trump's repeated deal claims as a running count ('for the 38th time'), framing credibility skepticism numerically rather than editorially.

Only outlet whose headline explicitly frames any deal outcome as a loss for Trump ('a Humiliation for Trump'), inverting the winner-framing that dominates Trump's own announcements and most other coverage.

bottom_line: The headline layer frames a war simultaneously declared 'over' by Trump and actively ongoing — with Hormuz drone battles, retaliatory missile strikes, and an unresolved oil-seizure threat — while Iran's denial of any final agreement is rendered as symmetrical 'he said / she said' rather than a corrective across 20 of 25 outlet-country buckets.
